ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

Downstream writers will use the query_postgres tool to inspect source data while implementing your plan.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 16, 2, 567528, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 16, 2, 567538, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 16, 2, 567663, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3912-d206-7579-9656-8fb25d5844be', conversation_id='019f3912-d206-7579-9656-8fb18df1e191')

data.overview: A global plan for mapping source data from `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` to the target Salesforce-like schema. The plan covers all five target tables: Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, and Installed_Asset__c. Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 16, 43, 698088, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: A global plan for mapping source data from `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` to the target Salesforce-like schema. The plan covers all five target tables: Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, and Installed_Asset__c. Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.
Source tables: fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset, fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account, fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project
Join keys: asset.client = account.id, asset.project = project.id
- Id <- asset.id (Direct map.)
- Name <- asset.name (COALESCE(TRIM(asset.name), 'Unknown Asset') as target 'Name' is NOT NULL.)
- Serial_Number__c <- asset.serial (Direct map. Apply TRIM().)
- Warranty_End_Date__c <- asset.warranty (Parse 'asset.warranty' (text) into 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Handle multiple formats (e.g., 'DD.MM.YYYY', 'YYYYMMDD', 'MM/DD/YYYY') using COALESCE with TO_DATE. Prefer NULL for unparseable dates.)
- Account__c <- asset.client, account.id (Join 'asset.client' to 'account.id' to get the Salesforce Account Id ('account.id').)
- Project__c <- asset.project, project.id (Join 'asset.project' to 'project.id' to get the Salesforce Project Id ('project.id').)
- Legacy_Asset_ID__c <- asset.id (Direct map from source 'asset.id'.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Set to NULL.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Set to NULL.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0.)
Notes: Join 'asset' with 'account' on `client` for `Account__c` and with 'project' on `project` for `Project__c`. Parse `warranty` date from text to YYYY-MM-DD.

Follow the plan. {{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    TRIM(asset.id) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(asset.name), 'Unknown Asset') AS "Name",
    TRIM(asset.serial) AS "Serial_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Warranty_End_Date__c",
    TRIM(account.id) AS "Account__c",
    TRIM(project.id) AS "Project__c",
    TRIM(asset.id) AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'asset') }} AS asset
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account
    ON asset.client = account.id
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'project') }} AS project
    ON asset.project = project.id

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(p.name), 'Unknown Project') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'active' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE 'In Planning'
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    TO_CHAR(
        COALESCE(
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'DD.MM.YYYY') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'YYYYMMDD') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'MM/DD/YYYY') ELSE NULL END
        ),
        'YYYY-MM-DD'
    ) AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    a.id AS "Account__c",
    o.id AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'project') }} AS p
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS a
    ON p.client_id = a.id
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} AS o
    ON p.opportunity_ref = o.id

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 17, 51, 72249, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

# Transformation guidelines

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

Output schema for dbt model results: `r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2`

## Account
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    account.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(account.name), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    account.id AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Silver' THEN 'Silver'

        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.region)) AS "Region__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.industry)) AS "Industry",
    NULL AS "Website",
    NULL AS "BillingCity",
    NULL AS "BillingCountry",
    account.id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- account.id: Directly mapped from the source account ID.
  - Name <- account.name: Mapped from the source account name, with a default 'Unknown Account' for nulls to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- account.id: Populated from the source account ID.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- account.tier: Mapped from the source tier, normalized to target enum values (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum). NULL if no match.
  - Region__c <- account.region: Mapped from the source region, trimmed and capitalized.
  - Industry <- account.industry: Mapped from the source industry, trimmed and capitalized.

  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 as no source column is specified.
Reasoning: A global plan for mapping source data from `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` to the target Salesforce-like schema. The plan covers all five target tables: Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, and Installed_Asset__c. Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.
The model directly maps fields from the source `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account` table to the target `Account` schema. `Name` handles NOT NULL constraints with a COALESCE, and `Customer_Tier__c` maps source values to the defined enum with a CASE statement. Other fields are either direct mappings, transformations for normalization, or set to NULL/default values as per the plan.

## Contact

```sql
    TRIM(c.id) AS "Id",
```

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 17, 51, 72249, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Output schema for dbt model results: `r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2`
Output tables to inspect (quoted exactly): "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Account", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Contact", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Opportunity", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Project__c", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Installed_Asset__c".
Use query_postgres to inspect tables in that schema after dbt has run.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    account.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(account.name), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    account.id AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Silver' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Bronze' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.region)) AS "Region__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.industry)) AS "Industry",
    NULL AS "Website",
    NULL AS "BillingCity",
    NULL AS "BillingCountry",
    account.id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- account.id: Directly mapped from the source account ID.
  - Name <- account.name: Mapped from the source account name, with a default 'Unknown Account' for nulls to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- account.id: Populated from the source account ID.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- account.tier: Mapped from the source tier, normalized to target enum values (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum). NULL if no match.
  - Region__c <- account.region: Mapped from the source region, trimmed and capitalized.
  - Industry <- account.industry: Mapped from the source industry, trimmed and capitalized.
  - Website <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - BillingCity <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - BillingCountry <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.

```sql
SELECT
```

```sql
        WHEN o.stage = 'Value Proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'

        WHEN o.stage = 'Negotiation/Review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Closed Won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Closed Lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'

    END AS "StageName",

    o.amount AS "Amount",
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 17, 51, 72249, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema

## Installed_Asset__c

- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Output schema for dbt model results: `r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2`
Output tables to inspect (quoted exactly): "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Account", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Contact", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Opportunity", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Project__c", "r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2"."Installed_Asset__c".
Use query_postgres to inspect tables in that schema after dbt has run.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    account.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(account.name), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    account.id AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Silver' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Bronze' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.region)) AS "Region__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.industry)) AS "Industry",
    NULL AS "Website",
    NULL AS "BillingCity",
    NULL AS "BillingCountry",
    account.id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- account.id: Directly mapped from the source account ID.
  - Name <- account.name: Mapped from the source account name, with a default 'Unknown Account' for nulls to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- account.id: Populated from the source account ID.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- account.tier: Mapped from the source tier, normalized to target enum values (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum). NULL if no match.
  - Region__c <- account.region: Mapped from the source region, trimmed and capitalized.
  - Industry <- account.industry: Mapped from the source industry, trimmed and capitalized.
  - Website <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - BillingCity <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - BillingCountry <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- account.id: Populated directly from the source account ID for verification.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as no source column is specified.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 as no source column is specified.
Reasoning: A global plan for mapping source data from `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` to the target Salesforce-like schema. The plan covers all five target tables: Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, and Installed_Asset__c. Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.
The model directly maps fields from the source `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account` table to the target `Account` schema. `Name` handles NOT NULL constraints with a COALESCE, and `Customer_Tier__c` maps source values to the defined enum with a CASE statement. Other fields are either direct mappings, transformations for normalization, or set to NULL/default values as per the plan.

## Contact
```sql
情緒{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    TRIM(c.id) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1))) AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(
        INITCAP(TRIM(SUBSTRING(c.full_name FROM POSITION(' ' IN c.full_name) + 1))),
        INITCAP(TRIM(c.full_name)),
        'Unknown'
    ) AS "LastName",
    LOWER(TRIM(c.email)) AS "Email",
    NULL AS "Phone",
    NULL AS "Title",
    NULL AS "Role__c",
    NULL AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    TRIM(a.id) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(c.id) AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'contact') }} AS c
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS a
    ON TRIM(c.account_ref) = TRIM(a.id)
```

  - FirstName <- contact.full_name: Extracted as the first word from 'full_name', trimmed and title-cased.

  - Email <- contact.email: Directly mapped from source email, trimmed and lowercased.
  - Phone <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.
  - Title <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.
  - Role__c <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column or mapping specified.
  - Preferred_Language__c <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column or mapping specified.
  - AccountId <- contact.account_ref, account.id: Resolved by joining 'contact.account_ref' to 'account.id', providing the Salesforce-style Account Id.

  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 as there is no corresponding source column.

Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.
The model extracts and formats contact details from the source 'contact' table. It joins with the 'account' table to correctly map the 'AccountId'. First and Last Names are derived from 'full_name' with appropriate string manipulations and a default for 'LastName' to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.

## Installed_Asset__c
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    TRIM(asset.id) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(asset.name), 'Unknown Asset') AS "Name",
    TRIM(asset.serial) AS "Serial_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN asset.warranty ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(asset.warranty, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Warranty_End_Date__c",
    TRIM(account.id) AS "Account__c",
    TRIM(project.id) AS "Project__c",
    TRIM(asset.id) AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'asset') }} AS asset
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account
    ON asset.client = account.id
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'project') }} AS project
    ON asset.project = project.id

```
Column notes:

```sql
SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",

        WHEN o.stage = 'Prospecting' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Qualification' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Needs Analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Value Proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Id. Decision Makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Perception Analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Proposal/Price Quote' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Negotiation/Review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Closed Won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN o.stage = 'Closed Lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    o.amount AS "Amount",
    'USD' AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    a.id AS "AccountId",
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} AS o
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS a
ON
    REGEXP_REPLACE(o.customer_number, '^KD-', 'ACC-') = a.id
```

## Project__c
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(p.name), 'Unknown Project') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'active' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE 'In Planning'
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    TO_CHAR(
        COALESCE(
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'DD.MM.YYYY') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'YYYYMMDD') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'MM/DD/YYYY') ELSE NULL END
        ),
        'YYYY-MM-DD'
    ) AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    a.id AS "Account__c",
    o.id AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'project') }} AS p
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS a
    ON p.client_id = a.id
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} AS o
    ON p.opportunity_ref = o.id
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 17, 51, 72249, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

Output schema for dbt model results: `r98qwya81_plan_write_critique_missing_relations_v2`

Use query_postgres to inspect tables in that schema after dbt has run.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    account.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(account.name), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    account.id AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Silver' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Bronze' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(account.tier)) = 'Platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.region)) AS "Region__c",
    TRIM(INITCAP(account.industry)) AS "Industry",
    NULL AS "Website",
    NULL AS "BillingCity",
    NULL AS "BillingCountry",
    account.id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS account

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- account.id: Directly mapped from the source account ID.
  - Name <- account.name: Mapped from the source account name, with a default 'Unknown Account' for nulls to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- account.id: Populated from the source account ID.

Cross-table relationships are resolved by joining on natural keys from source tables, which then become the Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., source `account.id` serves as target `Account.Id` and is referenced by other tables). Enum values will be normalized and mapped to target domains, with NULL as a fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is NOT NULL, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed from various text formats into ISO YYYY-MM-DD, with NULL for unparseable entries. Amounts will be handled as double precision. Legacy ID columns (`Legacy_*__c`) will be populated directly from the respective source table's natural key (`id`). Columns without a direct source will be set to NULL or a meaningful default where constraints dictate.

## Contact
```sql
情緒{{ config(materialized='table') }}

    TRIM(c.id) AS "Id",
```

## Installed_Asset__c
```sql

SELECT
    TRIM(asset.id) AS "Id",
```

## Project__c
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(p.name), 'Unknown Project') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'active' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE 'In Planning'
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    TO_CHAR(
        COALESCE(
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'DD.MM.YYYY') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'YYYYMMDD') ELSE NULL END,
            CASE WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'MM/DD/YYYY') ELSE NULL END
        ),
        'YYYY-MM-DD'
    ) AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    a.id AS "Account__c",
    o.id AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'project') }} AS p
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} AS a
    ON p.client_id = a.id
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} AS o
    ON p.opportunity_ref = o.id
```
